title: rootless container fails: "Setup user: Invalid argument"
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

rootless podman fails with:
```
Storing signatures
Error: container create failed: container_linux.go:344: starting container process caused "setup user: invalid argument"
: internal libpod error
```


**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Set up pod, try to launch container, as non-root:

```
podman pod create -p 10051:10051 -p 6379:6379 -p 8080:80 --name instance
podman run -dt  --pod instance registry.gitlab.com/modioab/modio-api/redis:master
Error: container create failed: container_linux.go:344: starting container process caused "setup user: invalid argument"
: internal libpod error
```
2. selinux in permissive mode, running other rootless containers seems to work (

3.

**Describe the results you received:**

Error message


**Describe the results you expected:**
Launched redis inside pod

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**
```
Version:            1.1.2
RemoteAPI Version:  1
Go Version:         go1.11.5
Git Commit:         a95a49d3038462d033f84ac314ec8a3064a99cff
Built:              Tue Mar  5 19:10:31 2019
OS/Arch:            linux/amd64
```

comments.body: @haircommander @giuseppe Could you guys look into this?
please attach the output you get with `--log-level debug`
I guess the image is setting an user that is not available in the user namespace
Shouldn't that fail when the image is pulling?
"setup user: invalid argument" looks like an explicity `USER`, that could be different than UIDs/GIDs used by the image (e.g. files owned by root but readable by anyone)
@Spindel Could you check the image and see what user it is attempting to be run as?

RUN echo redis:x:100004:100:redis:/:/sbin/nologin >> /etc/passwdUSER
100004EXPOSE 6379CMD ["/usr/bin/redis-server", "/etc/redis.conf"]


On Wed, Mar 20, 2019 at 3:04 PM Daniel J Walsh <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> @Spindel <https://github.com/Spindel> Could you check the image and see
> what user it is attempting to be run as?
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.
>
>
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/containers/libpod/issues/2714#issuecomment-474843312>,
> or mute the thread
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/ABmI5jXyv8KlV-9gSZ1zcaIy83xH4fgRks5vYj_jgaJpZM4b-5JX>
> .
>
100004 is a way too big value, you either change it or allocate more subuids to your user. Via /etc/subuids and /etc/subgids

Right, thank you.

On Wed, Mar 20, 2019 at 5:21 PM Giuseppe Scrivano <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> 100004 is a way too big value, you either change it or allocate more
> subuids to your user. Via /etc/subuids and /etc/subgids
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/containers/libpod/issues/2714#issuecomment-474911680>,
> or mute the thread
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/ABmI5nIZL46ft95bOkneJiTDqKmB4oHnks5vYmAjgaJpZM4b-5JX>
> .
>

@giuseppe Is the 65000 UID limit documented anywhere? We ought to add it to the manpages.

Also, can we potentially catch this earlier than runc, so we can produce a more sensible error?
It is just what shadow utils allocates by default. Yes we can catch it earlier, we already do that when extracting an image.  I'll work on a patch
@giuseppe Sure, but the run manpage should probably mention the 65k max UID limit will exist for most default installations of rootless Podman, barring nonstandard shadowutils or manual modification

I would not just document 65k, I would tell them to look at the range of UIDs allocated to /etc/subuid.  I am proposing that eventually we want to use much smaller ranges.  If we were running as root it would be nice to handle this situation by seeing if the UID is outside the range and then just adding it to the range.
